title: Chain Recovery Takeover via Compromised Secondary Email Domain
narrative: Attacker gains control of victim's recovery email account (often older webmail services like Yahoo, AOL, or defunct ISP domains) and uses it to reset credentials on higher-value primary accounts. Pattern shows legitimate activity for 90-180 days on P_emaildomain (primary account), then sudden P_emaildomain change request from R_emaildomain (recovery email) that itself shows geographic mismatch. Within 6-18 hours post-reset, rapid card testing begins: 8-15 transactions of $1-$3 at different MIDs (ProductCD=W for digital wallets), followed by 2-4 higher-value purchases ($340-$890) at electronics/gift card MCCs. Key signature: R_emaildomain shows dist1 values >3000km from historical addr1, recovery email domain is legacy provider (emaildomain_suffix match to known-vulnerable list), and card1_txn_count_24hr spikes from 0.2 average to 12+ immediately after email change timestamp.
indicators: P_emaildomain change initiated from R_emaildomain with dist1 >3000km from user's historical addr1
R_emaildomain matches legacy provider list (Yahoo, AOL, ISP domains) with known credential stuffing exposure
card1_txn_count_24hr increases from baseline <1/day to 12+ transactions within 18 hours of email reset
Initial 8-15 micro-transactions ($1-$3, ProductCD=W) followed by 2-4 purchases $340-$890 at MCC 5732 or 5946
txn_hour concentration: 85%+ of new activity occurs during is_night=1 periods, opposite of 180-day historical pattern
card1_amt_zscore exceeds 4.5 for transactions initiated within 24 hours of P_emaildomain modification event
summary: A customer's primary email address is changed via a request from their recovery email (often Yahoo, AOL, or old ISP domains), followed within hours by a burst of small test charges and then several high-value electronics or gift card purchases—all from locations thousands of kilometers away from the user's normal address.
reasoning: Fraudsters compromise weak secondary email accounts to hijack higher-value primary accounts, then immediately test stolen payment methods with micro-transactions before cashing out on expensive items, exploiting the trust chain between recovery and primary emails.
indicator_explanations: Account email change originates from distant location never seen in user history
Recovery email sits on older platform repeatedly breached in credential dumps
Card suddenly fires off dozens of transactions after months of quiet usage
Attacker tests card with tiny charges then pivots to high-ticket electronics purchases
All suspicious activity happens overnight when legitimate user typically sleeps
Purchase amounts wildly exceed what this cardholder normally spends per transaction
